perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall meet with the Company on at least a monthly basis, or more often to the extent reasonably requested by the Company, to review historical sales results by Product SKU, forward-looking sales forecasts by Product SKU, current inventories of the Products on hand, production scheduled and to be scheduled of the Products, and any other matters as the parties shall determine. Such meetings shall take place at the offices of the Company (or, at the election of the Company, by video or telephone conference) no later than the fifteenth (15th) day of each calendar month during the Term of this Agreement or at such other time as the parties shall mutually agree.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor may, at its discretion, meet with the Company to review matters as the parties shall determine. The location and timing of such meetings shall be at the discretion of the Contractor.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates regular meetings to review sales, forecasts, and inventories. The modified text eliminates the mandatory nature of the meetings and gives the contractor full discretion, reducing the company's ability to oversee operations effectively. This introduces legal risk because, under New Jersey law, there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to a contract to cooperate in achieving the objectives of the contract. Reducing communication and oversight might violate this covenant if it hinders the company's ability to manage its business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under New Jersey Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396, 420, 690 A.2d 575, 586 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the requirement for regular meetings, which could impede the Company's ability to monitor the Contractor's performance and manage its business effectively. This lack of cooperation could be seen as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New Jersey law, particularly if the Contractor's actions hinder the Company from realizing the benefits of the outsourcing agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates regular meetings to review sales and inventories, while the changed text makes these meetings discretionary for the contractor. This change contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing because removing the mandatory meetings hinders the company's ability to monitor the contractor's performance and manage its business effectively, as the Law Explanation states. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that reduced communication and oversight might violate this covenant if it hinders the company's ability to manage its business. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where essential information (mandatory meetings) is removed, creating a legal loophole by potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

perturbation.original_text: All paper or electronic records, files, documents, work papers and other information in any form, whether marked "confidential" or not, provided by the Company, its employees, agents or Affiliates or generated pursuant to this Agreement shall remain the exclusive property of the Company (collectively, "Files and Work Papers"). During the Term of this Agreement and for a reasonable transitional period thereafter, Contractor shall maintain adequate levels of information technology system redundancy, security and emergency back-up as the Company may reasonably require to protect the Files and Work Papers of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: All records, files, documents, work papers, and other information generated pursuant to this Agreement shall be managed according to Contractor's standard business practices. Contractor shall maintain IT security and backups as it deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause ensures the company retains ownership and control over its records and that the contractor maintains IT infrastructure to protect these records. The modified version gives the contractor discretion over records management and IT security. This omission introduces risk under New Jersey's data protection laws, as the lack of specific requirements for data security and ownership could lead to breaches and non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Identity Theft Prevention Act, N.J.S.A. 56:8-161 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific requirements for data security and backups and giving the contractor discretion, the modified text increases the risk that the Company's data could be compromised, leading to a violation of New Jersey's data protection laws. N.J.S.A. 56:8-161 requires businesses to take reasonable measures to protect personal information from unauthorized access, which is undermined by the vagueness of the modified clause.

Rev. Stat. 2023 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2022 N.J. Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 56 - Trade Names, Trade-Marks and Unfair Trade Practices Section 56:8-161 - Definitions relative to security of personal information. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 56:8-161 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 56:8-161 Definitions relative to security of personal information. 10. As used in sections 10 through 15 of P.L.2005, c.226 (C.56:8-161 through C.56:8-166): "Breach of security" means unauthorized access to electronic files, media or data containing personal information that compromises the security, confidentiality or integrity of personal information when access to the personal information has not been secured by encryption or by any other method or technology that renders the personal information unreadable or unusable. Good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of the business for a legitimate business purpose is not a breach of security, provided that the personal information is not used for a purpose unrelated to the business or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. "Business" means a sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, association, or other entity, however organized and whether or not organized to operate at a profit, including a financial institution organized, chartered, or holding a license or authorization certificate under the law of this State, any other state, the United States, or of any other country, or the parent or the subsidiary of a financial institution. "Communicate" means to send a written or other t
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the company's explicit ownership of records and replaces specific IT security requirements with the contractor's discretion. This contradicts N.J.S.A. 56:8-161, as the original clause ensured better protection of personal information, while the modified version increases the risk of unauthorized access. Snippet 1 defines a "breach of security" as unauthorized access to electronic files containing personal information, which the original clause sought to prevent through specific security measures, now absent in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall maintain Products Liability Insurance and in an amount satisfactory to Contractor, under which Contractor is named as an additional insured. All insurance coverages are to be placed with insurers which have a Best's rating of no less than "A." Such insurance requirements shall be maintained during the Term and shall continue for a minimum of three years following termination of this Agreement. Failure to comply with the insurance requirements shall place Company in material breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall maintain Products Liability Insurance. Failure to comply with the insurance requirements may place Company in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the company to maintain product liability insurance in an amount satisfactory to the contractor and with a reputable insurer (Best's rating of A or better), with continuation for three years post-termination. The modified text removes the explicit requirements for the insurance amount, the Best's rating, and the duration post-termination. It also weakens the consequence of non-compliance. This introduces a legal risk because it fails to adequately protect the contractor against product liability claims, and may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under New Jersey Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396, 420, 690 A.2d 575, 586 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirements for the amount of product liability insurance and insurer rating, the modified text fails to ensure adequate protection for the Contractor against potential losses resulting from defective products, which is inconsistent with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New Jersey law. The contractor is entitled to reasonably expect adequate protection given the outsourcing arrangement, and the weakened insurance clause undermines that expectation.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific requirements for product liability insurance (amount, insurer rating, duration post-termination), which are critical to protect the Contractor. The changed text omits these specifics, weakening the protection and consequence of non-compliance. While specific laws are not provided in the snippets, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the importance of adequate protection for the contractor and the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which are undermined by the omission. Because a mandated consumer protection was omitted, the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction applies.
